preFix.title: Removing mould and mildew stains from wooden garden furniture
preFix.subtitle: Clean, bleach, and re-oil weathered garden furniture that has developed surface mould
preFix.excerpt: How to remove green mould, black mildew, and grey weathering from teak, hardwood, or softwood garden furniture using an oxalic acid wood cleaner, and restore the wood surface with teak oil or preservative finish.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS guidance on garden furniture maintenance (open access). Timber Research and Development Association (TRADA) wood care notes. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: Surface mould on garden furniture is common after a wet winter or damp storage.

removes staining more thoroughly than sanding alone and does not damage the grain the way bleach products can. On teak and iroko, it also reverses the grey patina from UV exposure, restoring the honey-brown colour before re-oiling.

Oxalic acid wood cleaner (powder mixed at approximately 60 g per litre of warm water, or a ready-mixed product). A stiff bristle scrubbing brush. Teak oil, Danish oil, or exterior wood preservative to finish. 120-grit sandpaper for any sanding required before oiling. Gloves and eye protection when handling the acid solution.
Step 1, brush off loose mould and debris

Brush all surfaces of the furniture with a stiff dry brush to remove loose mould, leaves, dirt, and cobwebs before applying any liquid. Wet application to a heavily fouled surface dilutes the cleaner and reduces its effect. Work outdoors or in a well-ventilated space.
Step 2, apply the oxalic acid solution

Mix the oxalic acid solution and apply it generously to all stained surfaces with the paintbrush. Work the solution into the grain with the stiff brush, paying particular attention to black or green patches. Allow to dwell for 10 to 15 minutes; the staining will visibly lighten. Apply a second coat to stubborn areas. Rinse the furniture thoroughly with clean water and allow it to dry fully, at least 48 hours in dry weather.

Once dry, assess the surface. For furniture that will be oiled or stained, a light sand with 120-grit paper along the grain, using the sanding block on flat surfaces and by hand around shaped sections, opens the wood grain and gives the oil something to penetrate. For furniture that will be painted, sand to 120 grit. Wipe away all sanding dust before finishing.

Apply teak oil or Danish oil with the paintbrush, working it into the grain generously. Wipe away any excess that has not been absorbed after 30 minutes, pooled oil that dries on the surface becomes sticky and attracts dirt. Apply a second coat after the first has dried. Re-oil annually or whenever the wood surface looks dry and begins to grey.

attrs.fix: Move the furniture to a sunnier, more ventilated position when not in use. Apply a fungicidal wood preservative rather than plain oil as the maintenance coat; it inhibits regrowth for a full season.
attrs.cause: The furniture is stored in a shaded, damp spot where mould spores recolonise quickly.
attrs.symptom: Black staining returns within a few weeks of cleaning

attrs.fix: Allow more drying time — teak in particular needs 48 hours in dry weather after washing before oil will penetrate. If old oil is the cause, sand back to bare wood with 120-grit paper before re-oiling.
attrs.cause: The wood was not fully dry after rinsing, or old oil from a previous season has formed a surface film.
attrs.symptom: Teak oil does not soak in and sits on the surface

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
